data.title: From real estate to the internet, where is the secret to wealth in the next decade?
data.description:
Author: Sleepy.txt

Every generation has its own wealth code.

In the 1970s, when the spring thunder of reform and opening up had just sounded, the wealth code was written in the factory buildings of township enterprises and on the construction blueprints of Shekou, Shenzhen. Daring to go into business and buying real estate in prime locations was the most certain thing in that era.

In the 1980s and 1990s, when the first email was sent from China and when Yinghaiwei's time tunnel opened, the wealth code was written in the ".com" suffix and in the sleepless lights of Zhongguancun. Buying shares of Tencent and Alibaba and jumping into the internet wave was the most exciting choice of that era.

So, when we arrive in 2026, and the post-00s and post-10s generations begin to take center stage, where will their wealth secrets lie? The answer may lie in the lifestyle of this generation. To know what will be most valuable ten years from now, look at these young people today—where have they truly channeled their passion and talent? This leads to our story today. This story concerns the changing wealth concepts of a generation and the answers to the business world in the next decade. And the protagonist of our story today is Roblox. It may look like a children's playground, but it is becoming a social university for this generation of young people to learn business, practice finance, and earn their first pot of gold. Let's forget about the "metaverse," a narrative that was once overhyped and is now considered dead by everyone, and look at the story unfolding on Roblox. Alex Hicks was only 13 when he first encountered Roblox. Like other kids, he played games and made friends there. But soon, he discovered that what attracted him most to the platform wasn't playing, but creating. A year later, at 14, he began experimenting with the tools provided by Roblox to create his first game. He's been doing this for ten years. During those ten years, he hasn't worked a single day at a game company, nor has he received any professional programming training. At Roblox, he learned how to design game mechanics, how to price virtual items, how to maintain user activity through updates, and even how to manage a development team. By 2020, at only 24 years old, he already owned an independent game studio called RedManta, with annual revenue exceeding $1 million. Hicks' story is not an isolated case. Before entering Duke University, 18-year-old Alex Balvanz and his friends created a cops-and-robbers game called *Jailbreak*. Within months of its release, the game earned him $300,000 for tuition and made him a millionaire two years later. Behind these stories lies Roblox becoming the most important financial education classroom for this generation of young people. Through direct business practice, it has helped tens of millions of teenagers understand for the first time what income, expenses, profit, and return on investment are. Here, a massive creator economy is rising at an unprecedented pace. According to Roblox's annual economic impact report released in September 2025, the platform paid out over $1 billion to creators in the year from March 2024 to March 2025, an increase of over 31% year-over-year. The top 1000 creators earn an average of around $1 million per year, with significant growth. Of course, any mature business ecosystem requires a large denominator. Data shows that over 99% of creators earn less than $1000 annually. This precisely constitutes a real, even somewhat brutal, competitive business environment. The first lesson children learn here might be the Pareto principle of market economics. For this generation, their first financial education didn't come from their parents or school, but from Roblox. The first money they earned there, the first business model they learned, and even their first business failure will be deeply imprinted on their minds. This raises a question that worries all traditional financial institutions: when this generation of young people grows up, when their financial education is completed in a virtual game world, what should we banks, still waiting in line in the real world, do? Banks are forced to enter the game. The managers of TD Bank in Canada have clearly also seen this unsettling future. In March 2025, one of North America's largest banks officially launched a financial education game called *Treat Island Tycoon* on Roblox. In this game, players take on the role of a young entrepreneur building their own ice cream empire on a virtual island. They need to earn money, decide how to spend it, and learn about saving and borrowing. The game is completely free and its purpose is purely educational. TD Bank's decision was based on a detailed survey, in which as many as 86% of parents believed that interactive games or animated videos were far more effective than traditional books in teaching financial knowledge. In an interview, Emily Ross, VP of TD Bank, frankly stated, "Virtual experiences are reshaping early childhood education. What we need to do is provide a safe and fun entry point for children to subtly acquire financial literacy, laying the foundation for their future financial success." To translate this PR statement: We must find this generation of children where they are, not wait for them to find us. Where users spend their time, that's where the gateway to wealth lies. When Roblox boasts over 150 million daily active users, spanning a wide age range from children to young adults, it's no longer just a gaming platform, but a battleground for the minds of the next generation of financial users. TD Bank's anxiety is a microcosm of the entire traditional financial industry. Moreover, their competitors are no longer just other banks. Around the time TD Bank launched its game, the American fintech company Chime partnered with the beauty brand e.l.f. Beauty to launch another financial education game on Roblox. Even a beauty brand is vying for a foothold in financial education, which means the boundaries of financial services are being completely broken down, and the starting point of the competition has shifted to 10-year-old children. Traditional banks once believed their moats were physical branches, licenses, and substantial capital. But now they've discovered that their true moat may simply be the user's mindset. And the user's mindset is shaped during adolescence. When a child becomes accustomed to managing their assets, making transactions, and investing in the virtual world, how much demand will they have for physical banks when they grow up? When his first income comes from selling a virtual item he designed, instead of the New Year's money from his parents, how will his definition of assets change? This touches upon a deeper question: when the setting for financial literacy shifts from bank lobbies to the gaming world, we will be facing a group of digital natives with completely different concepts of wealth. How will they reshape the future financial world? When Virtual Assets Become "For the First Time" To understand the impending changes, we must first clarify a core difference: The relationship between this generation of young people and their parents regarding virtual assets has undergone a fundamental qualitative shift. Many might say that trading game items is nothing new, and it wasn't invented by Roblox. Indeed, those born in the 80s and 90s traded gold in World of Warcraft and bought and sold equipment in Fantasy Westward Journey. But the virtual asset trading of that time was fundamentally different from what we see today. During the heyday of World of Warcraft, Blizzard officially prohibited any form of cash transaction and ruthlessly banned accounts belonging to gold farming studios; players would even spontaneously hunt them down. Virtual asset trading was an underground black market suppressed by the official authorities. However, on Roblox, it was a paved road paved by the official authorities. You create content, players consume Robux to purchase it, and then you exchange this virtual currency for real US dollars through DevEx (the developer redemption program). This is the platform's core and most encouraged business model. Roblox's CEO even proudly announced in earnings calls that they had paid out over $1 billion to creators that year. The definition of assets among gamers has undergone tremendous changes with the development of the virtual world and games. In CS:GO, someone might offer $1.5 million for a rare weapon, and the weapon's owner might refuse to sell, deeming the offer too low. The entire CS:GO market capitalization has now reached $5.8 billion, resembling a massive independent economic entity. Players analyze skin price trends like they analyze stocks, even engaging in financial operations such as manipulation and short selling. A single game update once caused the market capitalization to evaporate by over $2 billion in a short period, a devastating event comparable to a small financial crisis. While those born in the 1970s were still teaching their children how to save money, those born in the 2000s had already learned how to trade in CS:GO. This difference is shaping two generations with drastically different financial worldviews. A 2025 survey showed that 51% of Generation Z in the United States own or have owned cryptocurrency, and a staggering 45% hoped to receive cryptocurrency as a Christmas gift. Meanwhile, the percentage of Gen Z users with traditional bank accounts has fallen below 50%. For them, the cash in their digital wallets, the skins in CS:GO, and the Robux in Roblox are essentially no different from the savings in their bank accounts. They are all digital, and can all be used for payments, transactions, and investments. The distinction between virtual and reality has long since blurred in their eyes. A report released by Roblox at the end of 2025 showed that as many as 70% of Gen Z users said that their virtual avatars' clothing on the platform directly influenced their shopping decisions and style preferences in the real world. The aesthetics of the virtual world are spilling over into the real world. Virtual assets are becoming real because the definition of "real" itself has been rewritten by this generation. The Invisible Inertia: Why do each generation's wealth ultimately end up heavily invested in the areas they were most familiar with in their youth? This is no coincidence; behind it are three invisible hands secretly orchestrating events. The first hand is cognitive lock-in. Legendary investor Peter Lynch famously proposed the investment principle: "Invest in what you know." The underlying logic of this principle is that people are naturally inclined to make decisions and investments in areas they are most familiar with, because familiarity brings a sense of security and reduces uncertainty. For those born in the 1970s, their tangible homes are the most certain assets. For those born in the 1980s, the internet products they use daily are the most credible investment targets. And for those born in the 2000s, they spend even more time in the virtual world than in the physical world. Virtual items that can be traded, flaunted, and generate social value are, for them, no less real and certain than a property in their parents' eyes. This kind of perception, rooted in their upbringing, is difficult to change once formed, and it will continue to guide their wealth flow for decades to come. The second factor is the intergenerational transfer of trust. For those born in the 1970s, trust was built on the nation and the land; a red property certificate was their ultimate source of security. For those born in the 1980s, trust began to shift towards business organizations and legal contracts; an option agreement bearing the company logo represented the right to share in the future value of a business organization. And for those born in the 2000s, the foundation of trust is shifting from authoritative institutions to virtual online consensus. They believe in code, algorithms, and the scarcity recognized by millions of players worldwide. A hash value recorded on the blockchain, or a skin with extremely low availability on CS:GO's global servers, carries a credibility in their eyes even greater than a prospectus for a financial product issued by a bank. The third force is the self-realization of network effects. When a generation collectively invests its attention, time, and money in an emerging field, a powerful network effect is created. The more people participate, the higher the value of that field; the higher the value, the more talent and capital are attracted, forming a positive cycle that ultimately self-realizes as the next era's wealth hotspot. The golden two decades of real estate and the entrepreneurial wave of the internet both followed this path. Today, hundreds of millions of young people are building new social networks, new economic systems, and new cultural identities in the virtual world. This force, formed by collective consensus, is laying the most solid foundation for the value of digital assets. Understanding these three principles is crucial to understanding why some US stock traders still believe Roblox is severely undervalued after the bursting of the metaverse bubble. In their eyes, Roblox is no longer just a simple gaming company, but an gateway to the future world of wealth, driven by cognitive lock-in, trust transfer, and network effects. The Mislabeled Company For a long time, people have been accustomed to measuring Roblox by the standards of game companies, comparing it to traditional gaming giants like Activision Blizzard and EA. However, the value of a financial infrastructure cannot be measured by the standards of a game company. Roblox's core business model is not about making and selling games, but about providing a complete, closed-loop economic system. In this system, it plays four key roles: First, it is the creator of the world. It provides the underlying physics engine, development tools, and servers, allowing creators to build their own virtual worlds at low cost, like building with Lego bricks. Second, it is the central bank. It issues and manages the only universal currency in this world, Robux. It determines the amount of Robux issued, the inflation rate, and most importantly, the exchange rate between Robux and the real-world currency (the US dollar). Third, it is the tax authority and payment gateway. For every transaction that occurs on the platform, whether a user buys virtual items or a developer withdraws Robux, Roblox takes a percentage as a fee. It processes hundreds of millions of micro-transactions every day, highly similar to Alipay or WeChat Pay. Fourth, it is the market regulator. It is responsible for reviewing all content on the platform, combating fraud and illegal activities, and maintaining the stability and fairness of the entire economic system. These four roles together constitute a typical platform economy. It does not directly produce goods (games), but profits by setting rules, providing services, and collecting taxes. This is exactly the same business logic as platforms like Alibaba and Amazon. From this perspective, Roblox's payment of over $1 billion to creators in 2025 has a completely different meaning. This is no longer "the cost of a game company," but rather "the disposable income of residents" in a vast economy. The economic activity generated by the entire platform is more like a country's GDP. However, why is such a vast economy still continuously losing money? This is precisely the root of the market's confusion about it and the core reason why it is underestimated. Roblox's losses are structural and deliberately chosen. Its revenue and cost structure is completely different from traditional companies. For every $1 spent, approximately 49% of the money is spent before Roblox can recognize it as revenue. Of this, 22% goes to app stores like Apple and Google as channel fees, and another 27% goes directly to creators. The remaining money is used to cover server, R&D, and management costs. This model is unacceptable to investors seeking short-term profits. However, it becomes easier to understand if compared to the early days of Alipay. Alipay also suffered huge losses for many years in its early years because it bore all the costs of infrastructure construction and user education, cultivating a generation's mobile payment habits through massive subsidies. When everyone gets used to paying with QR codes, and when it becomes an indispensable infrastructure of the entire commercial society, its trillion-dollar value will truly be realized. Of course, Roblox and Alipay are still fundamentally different. The former is rooted in entertainment scenarios, while the latter addresses the basic need for payment. However, they are strikingly similar in their strategic logic of exchanging losses for generational habits. The receding tide of the metaverse is actually a good thing for Roblox. It washes away the speculative bubble, preventing people from confusing it with empty concepts, thus allowing them to see its true value: a financial infrastructure rooted in the daily lives of hundreds of millions of young people, possessing a powerful network effect and a closed-loop economic system. The Answer to the Next Decade Financial education for an era is shifting from the physical world to the digital world. When a generation's financial enlightenment begins with business practices in a virtual world; when their first asset is a tradable game skin; when their trust in digital wallets surpasses their reliance on physical banks, a completely new economic paradigm has begun. How we understand platforms like Roblox today will determine how we understand the business transformation of the next decade. When a company's users are simultaneously its consumers, producers, promoters, and investors, are traditional valuation models still effective? When a company's core product is not a commodity, but a set of economic rules and a circulating currency, how should we define its boundaries? The answers to these questions cannot be found in today's financial statements. However, the hundreds of millions of young people creating, trading, and socializing in the virtual world are converging into a powerful force reshaping the future business landscape. Historical experience has repeatedly proven that understanding what young people define as assets is key to understanding the future flow of wealth. The wealth code of each generation is written in every new consensus reached by that generation.

data.title: Bitcoin and the New World Order
data.description:
Author: Arch; Source: X, @ArchLending; Translated by: Shaw & Jinse Finance

For years, Bitcoin holders have been accused of acting prematurely, being paranoid, or chasing a speculative fantasy. Just days ago, the world's most influential leader, at the Munich Security Conference, effectively endorsed this view.

Ray Dalio, in an article now widely circulated online, explained bluntly: The world order established after 1945 has officially collapsed.

The old rules no longer apply, and for those who have been holding Bitcoin and wondering when the world will see what they've seen…

That moment has arrived.

This moment has arrived.

Every monetary system has its expiration date. History tells us that monetary systems do not last forever, but evolve along with the geopolitical order that underpins them. The gold standard gave way to the Bretton Woods system; the Bretton Woods system gave way to the petrodollar system. Each transition was chaotic, controversial, and only becomes apparent in hindsight. We may now be in one such transition period.

Fiscal deficits are widening in major economies, and central banks are grappling with a situation where the tools they have relied on for decades (low interest rates, quantitative easing, forward guidance) are becoming less effective while their side effects are increasing.

Meanwhile, currency itself is being used as a geopolitical tool in ways unimaginable to the previous generation. The freezing of Russia's central bank reserves in 2022 was a watershed moment because it revealed structural problems. Any country's reserves, if held within another country's financial system, could be cut off overnight. This understanding has reached not only Moscow, but also Riyadh, New Delhi, and the finance ministries of all sovereign nations. Bitcoin was not born for the old world. People often discuss Bitcoin from a price perspective, but its deeper significance lies in structural factors. It is the first monetary asset in history that is not controlled by any single country, institution, or alliance. In the old order, characterized by relatively stable alliances, predictable monetary policies, and widespread trust in institutions, these characteristics were theoretically interesting but not urgent. In this new order, defined by fragmented alliances, competing economic blocs, and the active weaponization of financial infrastructure, these characteristics become extremely useful. Bitcoin is a means of combating systemic instability stemming from the rewriting of the rules of the game. What forward-thinking holders are considering: The emerging world order creates a set of conditions favorable to assets with specific characteristics: Cross-border portability; Unmanipulated scarcity; Settlement without intermediaries; Neutrality unaffected by the foreign policy of any single government. Gold has played these roles for millennia, so it's no surprise that central banks have significantly increased their gold reserves in recent years. However, gold has limitations in a digital, interconnected world because it's difficult to transfer quickly, verification is costly, and it's impossible to send it over the internet in seconds. Bitcoin addresses all these limitations while retaining the core attribute that gives gold its enduring value: the enforced credible scarcity through means other than political will. For investors focused on long-term investments, the question is shifting from "Will Bitcoin rise?" to "In a world with a volatile monetary system, where should I store value to ensure no single actor can destroy it?" The transition will not be comfortable, but that doesn't mean the road ahead will be smooth. Geopolitical transitions are inherently volatile. Short-term declines, regulatory uncertainty, and shifting narratives are characteristic of these periods. However, it's necessary to distinguish between asset volatility and systemic fragility. Bitcoin is highly volatile, but as world leaders now acknowledge, the traditional financial order is fragile. These are two completely different things. Those who can successfully navigate this period are likely to be those who understand this distinction and can tolerate short-term discomfort, because they see a longer-term trend. Looking to the future, old assumptions such as the dollar's hegemonic status being taken for granted, stable alliances, and monetary policy being apolitical are being re-examined globally, and the final outcome is still being written. But one thing is becoming increasingly clear: in a world that is gradually moving away from centralized trust and towards multipolar competition, assets that do not require trust in any single counterparty are forward-looking assets. The world order is changing. A question worth considering is whether your positioning reflects the world of the past or the world that is forming.

data.title: From Spring Festival Gala robots to the computing power energy war: Why China holds the trump card in the AI ​​era
data.description:
During the Spring Festival of 2026, while the world was still marveling at OpenAI's latest model parameters, China showcased another side of AI to the world through a Spring Festival Gala—the physical application of embodied intelligence.

Looking at the program list for the 2026 CCTV Spring Festival Gala, we see an unprecedented "AI parade." This is no longer the simple robotic dance display of a few years ago, but a concentrated explosion of China's robotics industry across "multiple enterprises, multiple models, and all scenarios."

Magic Atom's full-stack cluster made the robots the best "atmosphere team," dancing alongside Jordan Chan and Jackson Yee in "Intelligent Manufacturing Future," their coordination so perfect it was hard to tell they were real.

Unitree's G1 and H2 robots demonstrated astonishing motion control capabilities in "War BOT"—not real-time remote control, but entirely autonomous balancing via edge computing power.

data.title: The Evolution of the Coin Listing Cycle: Yesterday's Wind Can't Make Today's Kite Fly
data.description:
Author: danny；Source: X，@agintender

If we compare the crypto industry to a set of teeth, then the path of coin listings over the years is like a process of "industry orthodontics." From the chaos of 2017 to the industrialized, streamlined distribution of tokens in 2025, each token allocation method in the market is essentially a correction of the industry's structural abnormalities and a challenge to the token structure.

In this process, the path taken by project teams to pursue top-tier liquidity has evolved from the early "voice-driven game" to the current "exorbitant dowry model" of offering huge sums of money; exchanges, in order to survive and acquire traffic and transaction fees, have also shifted from the early listing logic to the pricing logic. How exchanges, project teams, VCs, and traders destroy and love each other; slander and achieve each other. For you, a thousand times over. Introduction Teeth are a very "magical" organ in the human body. Why? Because teeth are the only organ in the human body that, after adulthood, allows us to deeply customize, move, and modify them through physical and biological means. This "plasticity" allows us to combat genetic misalignment and the wear and tear and discomfort caused by aging. We usually think of bones as hard and fixed, and that teeth, growing in the alveolar bone, should remain motionless. However, orthodontics (wearing braces) utilizes the characteristic of bone as a "dynamic and active tissue." When braces continuously apply a constant, light force to the teeth, the alveolar bone on the side of the tooth under pressure feels the stress, and the body sends out osteoclasts to absorb the bone in that area, making way for the tooth; while on the side where the tooth moves, the body sends out osteoblasts to fill the gap with new bone. The teeth simultaneously "break down" and "rebuild" bone, thus achieving slow movement within the skeleton. This is something that no other hard organ in the human body can do. After all, assuming you're not exceptionally gifted, you can't shorten your thigh bone or reposition your ribs by applying pressure, but you can with your teeth. The same applies to the rules and policies for listing on cryptocurrency exchanges. Part One: Listing on Cryptocurrencies = The Struggle and Transfer of Asset Pricing Power. This article divides the path of cryptocurrency listing into four stages: deciduous teeth – teething – malformation – orthodontics. The core issue running through these four stages is: who controls the pricing power of the asset. **Phase 1 (Community Pricing)** Pricing power was held by "market promoters" and grassroots communities. Traffic was king; whoever had the loudest voice was right. The result was that bad money drove out good, and the market was filled with noise. **Phase 2 (Exchange Pricing)** Exchanges regained pricing power through IEOs/Launchpads, acting as "gatekeepers" and "investment banks." The exchange's reputation became the core support for asset prices. The third stage (the collapse of VC pricing) saw VCs wield excessive pricing power in the primary market, making the secondary market unprofitable. Exchanges were forced to intervene, attempting to "rob the rich to help the poor" through coercive measures (Airdrop), but this was merely a painkiller, not a fundamental solution. The fourth stage (marketization/derivatives pricing) saw the funds for market speculation no longer in the spot market, thus transferring pricing power to more mature financial mechanisms. Through "contract trading" and pre-market trading, the market formed a fair price after sufficient competition, no longer relying on a single narrative or VC valuation reports. Part Two: The Historical Context, Logic, and Evolution of Crypto Listing Phase One: 2017-2018 "Baby Teeth Stage"—A Wilderness Era Where Voice Matters Core Path: Direct Listing, Community Voting During this period, the industry was in a "dentistless" state. The listing logic was heavily influenced by founder and community sovereignty; as long as a project could attract followers, it could gain entry. This was the "genesis" stage of Crypto. The industry was still in the era of pure trading platforms, with users primarily focused on the convenience, speed, and low cost of transactions. At the time, most mainstream exchanges were slow and unstable. New platforms built their reputation through "extreme simplicity," lacking complex learning systems or social features; their interfaces were designed entirely for experienced professional traders. **Reasons:** Customer Acquisition Anxiety: Startups needed to attract traffic from competitors quickly and cost-effectively. "Community voting" was not just about selecting coins, but also a battle for community belonging. **Compliance Vacuum:** Global regulators had not yet intervened, giving exchanges extremely high decision-making freedom. The logic was extremely simple: whoever had more fans guaranteed liquidity. **Method:** Represented by Binance's "monthly community voting for coin listings," users could vote by paying a small amount of tokens (e.g., 0.1 BNB). Winning projects (such as Zilliqa and Pundi X) gained top-tier traffic almost for free, but vote manipulation caused severe market misalignment, ultimately forcing them to abandon the program. Phase Two: 2019-2022 "Teething Period"—Ecosystem Building and Premium Issuance Core Path: IEO (Initial Public Offering), Launchpad, Launchpool, Direct Listing The industry began to adopt a corrective mechanism called "ecosystem." Exchanges were no longer just intermediaries, but "dentists" with in-depth due diligence capabilities. Background of the Era After the ICO bubble burst in 2017, fraud and technical vulnerabilities tarnished the industry's credibility. The market needed a safer and more reputable fundraising method. Meanwhile, the arrival of DeFi Summer (2020) made "liquidity mining" an industry consensus.

Reasons

Credit Repair: Exchanges, through Launchpad, introduced "bank-level" due diligence, acting as industry dentists to screen projects with legitimate teams and technology, upgrading the ICO model to the more secure IEO (Initial Exchange Offering).

Ecosystem Closed Loop: To solidify user stickiness, platforms forcibly empowered their own ecosystem tokens (such as BNB) through Launchpool, allowing users to obtain new tokens through "holding" rather than "buying," reducing participation risk.

2019-2020 (IPO Frenzy):

Launchpad (such as Bittorrent) introduced a pricing issuance model. Project teams not only had to pass technical reviews but also accept the exchange's pricing "suggestions" to ensure a certain "wealth effect" after listing.

2021-2022 (Lock-up Empowerment): Launchpool became mainstream, empowering platform tokens and marking a shift from "buying new tokens" to "mining new tokens." Users locked up platform tokens to receive new token distributions, forcibly binding project interests to the platform ecosystem. Third Stage: 2023-2024 "Abnormal Period"—The Game of High Valuation and Low Circulation and Mechanism Upgrades Core Path: HODLer Airdrop, Launchpool Background: Venture capital (VC) returned to the market on a large scale, giving rise to a large number of projects with valuations of hundreds of millions of US dollars but extremely low circulating supply (median only 12.3%). This structure leaves retail investors with virtually no profit potential in the secondary market, only continuous selling pressure from unlocked tokens. Simultaneously, hefty fines and CZ's imprisonment force a shift in focus from "wild growth" to "global compliance and stability."

Reasons

Pricing power conflict: VC-driven projects peak upon launch, depriving the market of price discovery. Exchanges, to protect their ecosystem, must use forceful measures to correct this, "returning profits to the people."

Compliance pressure: Starting in May 2024, rules explicitly favored small and medium-sized projects with high distribution ratios, requiring project teams to reduce the float portion, aiming to combat VC manipulation of pricing.

Corrective measures: The introduction of HODLer Airdrop and Megadrop for long-term holders forcibly distributed the "gift" directly to retail investors.

This is the most painful "periodontalgia" stage in the industry's orthodontic process. VCs have spawned a large number of projects that "peak immediately upon launch," with the median circulating supply of tokens falling to 12.3%. Binance's industry report shows that new projects in 2024 alone have a potential selling pressure of approximately $155 billion in the next 12-24 months. Due to VC manipulation of pricing, retail investors bought at high prices, resulting in a peak upon listing and severely damaging market confidence. Poor performance in the secondary market led to a contraction in spot trading volume. To maintain the attractiveness of the platform token, attract users, and meet trading demand, the platform began to massively implement HODLer Airdrop (airdrops for long-term holders) and Megadrop (distribution combined with Web3 tasks). Listing policies gradually shifted towards smaller projects with higher distribution ratios. Starting in the second half of 2024, exchange contract mechanisms underwent a major upgrade, supporting a wider range of perpetual contracts for smaller and newer cryptocurrencies. This allowed for risk hedging and early pricing through derivatives before spot liquidity matured. Exchanges also shifted their traffic and revenue sources towards perpetual contract trading. Phase Four: 2025 "Orthogonalization Period"—A Multi-Level, Industrialized Compliance Matrix Core Path: Binance Alpha Airdrop, Pre-Market Trading, Web3 Wallet Integration Background: 2025 is considered the "Year Zero of Cryptocurrency Industrialization." The total market capitalization of digital assets surpassed $4 trillion, with Bitcoin becoming a macro asset. Perpetual contracts have become dominant in the derivatives market, accounting for over 75% of global crypto derivatives trading volume.

Reasons

Pricing power shifts: The market is no longer driven by narratives and hype, but by ETF traffic, corporate financial reports, and protocol revenue.

Efficiency optimization: Futures First allows pricing of new coins through derivatives before they are listed on the spot market. Data from 2025 shows that the conversion cycle for this path has been shortened to 14 days, making it the fastest way to enter the mainstream market.

Pre-Market: This is the most important mechanism change in 2025. The introduction of "Pre-Market" allows users to trade perpetual contracts with up to 5x leverage based on external price feeds before the token is officially listed on the spot market.

Pre-Market: This is the most important mechanism change in 2025. It allows users to trade perpetual contracts with up to 5x leverage based on external price feeds before the token is officially listed on the spot market.

Pre-Market: This allows users to trade perpetual contracts with up to 5x leverage before the token is officially listed on the spot market.

... Deep Liquidity for Smaller Cryptocurrencies: The massive traffic attracted by contract trading and pre-market trading has drawn numerous small and medium-sized investors, significantly increasing the trading potential and liquidity of smaller cryptocurrency contracts. This allows new coins like ESP, AZTEC, and KITE, which haven't yet launched spot trading, to quickly establish derivatives liquidity, becoming the fastest path to mainstream adoption. The average time from launch to official token issuance is approximately 14 days. Binance Alpha (2.0): As a "pre-launch token selection pool," projects must first "level up" here, proving their performance in the secondary market (including price movements and trading volume) before gradually upgrading from contract trading to spot trading. Part Three: The Power Shift from "Grassroots" to "Industrial Orthodontics"

Stage One: The Grassroots Era of "Voice is Justice" (2017-2018)

This was the period of "primitive accumulation" for exchanges. They had almost no ability to discern the quality of projects, nor did they need to.

After watching the movie, come back to read this article; your feelings may be different—what does it mean to destroy and make each other? What does it mean to be a good person again?

data.title: Dalio's lengthy article: The old order is dead; trade wars and capital wars will become the norm.
data.description: <div class="share___SVvtl"><div>Ray Dalio, founder of Bridgewater Associates, the world's largest hedge fund, released a major article on February 14th, officially declaring that the world has entered the sixth stage of the "Great Cycle"—a period without rules, rife with chaos, and where might makes right. Dalio's core argument is that the world order established after World War II in 1945 has completely collapsed, and conflicts between major powers will no longer be bound by international law, but will revert to primitive power struggles. He warns that this stage is typically accompanied by a mix of internal turmoil and external wars, until a new order is established through conflict. According to the latest developments cited by Dalio, at the Munich Security Conference held on February 14, 2026, major global leaders reached a rare consensus on the "end of the old order." German Chancellor Merz stated bluntly that "the world order that has lasted for decades no longer exists," and pointed out that freedom is no longer a given in this new era. French President Macron echoed this assessment, warning that Europe's old security architecture has failed and that preparations must be made for war. US Secretary of State Marco Rubio explicitly stated that the world has entered a "new geopolitical era." Dalio points out that, in this phase, international relations will follow the "law of the jungle." Unlike states that have police and judges, the international system lacks a powerful supranational institution to adjudicate disputes. When major powers clash, they do not seek legal solutions but rather resort to threats or war. This means that trade wars, technology wars, geopolitical wars, and capital wars will become the norm and may eventually escalate into military conflict. For capital markets, this marks the beginning of an extremely uncertain period. Dalio emphasizes that history shows that the risk of war is highest when two opposing major powers are militarily evenly matched and have irreconcilable existential differences. Investors must realize that in this phase, economic instruments will be fully weaponized, traditional hedging logic may fail, and the transfer of wealth and power will occur dramatically. In his article, Dalio details five main forms of conflict between nations: trade/economic warfare, technology warfare, geopolitical warfare, capital warfare, and military warfare. He points out that the first four types of warfare typically exist as intense competitive means, escalating long before the outbreak of hot war. Currently, the world is in a typical dilemma of great power rivalry—the "prisoner's dilemma." Neither side can be certain that the other will not attack, thus falling into a cycle of tit-for-tat escalation. Dalio's analysis suggests that while most competition aims for wealth and power, once this competition escalates into military conflict, conflicts across all dimensions are amplified. He warns in the article that the key to "winning" a war lies in achieving objectives without sacrificing core interests, but history is often filled with "foolish wars" caused by misjudgment and emotional decision-making. A Historical Mirror: Economic Warfare and Markets in the 1930s The article provides a reference for the current situation by providing a detailed review of pre-World War II history. Dalio points out that the Great Depression of the 1930s exacerbated wealth conflicts in various countries, leading to the rise of populism, authoritarianism, and nationalism. Whether it was the fascistization of Germany and Japan, or the increased trade protectionism in the United States and Britain, these were extreme reactions to the economic crisis. Dalio specifically mentions that economic and capital warfare had already begun in the decade leading up to the hot war. For example, the United States initiated a trade war in 1930 through the Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act, and subsequently imposed a devastating oil embargo and asset freeze on Japan on the eve of World War II. This economic "strangulation" forced Japan to choose between compromise and war. From a market perspective, the German stock market initially rose due to military victories during the early stages of the war, but ultimately returned to zero following defeat; while the US stock market strengthened, stimulated by massive government spending and war-related needs. This demonstrates that market performance during wartime is highly dependent on a nation's chances of winning on the battlefield. Capital Warfare: Asset Freezes and Market Blockades In the current "sixth phase," the tools of capital warfare are being frequently mentioned and used. Dalio listed three classic capital warfare tactics: **Asset Freezing/Seizure:** Preventing an adversary from using or selling foreign assets they rely on, in extreme cases including unilateral debt default or direct asset confiscation. **Cutting Off Capital Market Access:** Prohibiting an adversary from accessing capital markets in their own country or a third country for financing. Embargoes and Blockades: Cutting off trade flows of critical goods (such as energy and technology). Dalio warns that, **because might makes right, the strong often exploit the weaknesses of their opponents for plunder.** In the event of escalating conflict, the security of traditional financial assets will face enormous challenges under such sanctions. The Logic of Wealth During Wartime Dalio concludes by analyzing the economic policies and wealth protection logic in a wartime environment. He points out that governments typically implement strict controls during wartime, including rationing, price controls, capital controls, and asset confiscation. To finance the war, governments issue large amounts of debt and monetize it, leading to currency devaluation. Therefore, Dalio advises investors to be extremely cautious about debt assets during this period. Historical experience shows that the best store of wealth during wartime is usually gold, because credit is often unacceptable or significantly devalued during wartime. He concluded that while the rise and fall of great powers is an inevitable part of history, the trauma of cyclical evolution can be managed if power is used wisely and productivity is maintained. However, in the present moment, the world must prepare for the impending upheaval and the painful adjustment to a new order. The following is the original text: Official Announcement: The World Order Has Collapsed Ray Dalio At the Munich Security Conference, the world order after 1945 was declared dead by most leaders, and the picture behind it is presented in the 2026 Security Report entitled "Deconstruction". More specifically, German Chancellor Friedrich Merz stated, "The world order that lasted for decades no longer exists," and we are in an era of "power politics." He explicitly stated that in this new era, freedom "is no longer taken for granted." French President Emmanuel Macron echoed Merz's assessment, stating that the old security architecture linking Europe to the previous world order no longer exists, and Europe must prepare for war. US Secretary of State Marco Rubio stated that we are in a "new geopolitical era" because the "old world" has disappeared. In my words, we are in the sixth phase of a great cycle, characterized by massive disorder stemming from a period without rules, where might makes right, and great power conflict. How this sixth phase operates is explained in detail in Chapter Six, "External Order and the Great Cycle of Disorder," of my book, *Principles: Coping with the Changing World Order*. Given the now almost universal consensus that the post-1945 world order has collapsed and we are entering a new one, I think it's worth your time to read this. Chapter Six: The Great Cycle of External Order and Disorder The relationships between people and the order that governs them, whether internal or external, operate in essentially the same way and are intertwined. In fact, not long ago, there was no distinction between internal and external order because there were no clearly defined and mutually recognized boundaries between states. Therefore, the six stages of the cycle from order to disorder within a state that I described in the previous chapter also apply to relations between states, with one important exception: international relations are more subject to naked power dynamics. This is because all governance systems require effective and agreed-upon 1) laws and legislative capacity, 2) law enforcement capacity (e.g., police), 3) adjudication methods (e.g., judges), and 4) clear, specific, and enforceable consequences appropriate to the offense (e.g., fines and imprisonment). These elements are either absent or less effective in guiding relations between states than in guiding domestic relations. While attempts have been made to make the external order more rule-abiding (e.g., through the League of Nations and the United Nations), they have generally failed because these organizations do not possess more wealth and power than the most powerful states. When individual states possess more power than the collective of states, the more powerful individual states dominate. This is because power is supreme, and equal wealth and power are rarely relinquished without struggle. When powerful nations clash, they don't have lawyers present their cases to judges. Instead, they threaten each other with either a deal or war. The international order follows the law of the jungle, not international law. There are five main types of conflict between states: trade/economic warfare, technology warfare, capital warfare, geopolitical warfare, and military warfare. Trade/economic warfare primarily refers to conflicts over tariffs, import/export restrictions, and other ways to economically harm competitors. Technology warfare concerns which technologies can be shared and which are considered national security protections. Geopolitical warfare refers to conflicts over territory and alliances; these conflicts are typically resolved through negotiation and explicit or implicit commitments, rather than combat. Capital warfare is a conflict imposed through financial instruments, such as sanctions, cutting off funding and credit by punishing institutions and governments that provide capital and credit, or restricting foreign access to domestic capital markets. Military warfare, on the other hand, involves actual firing and the deployment of troops. Most struggles between states fall into one or more of these categories (for example, cyber warfare has a place in all categories). These struggles concern wealth and power, and the ideologies associated with them. While most of these wars do not involve firing and killing, they are power struggles. In most cases, the first four types of warfare evolve over time into fierce competition between rival states, until military war begins. These struggles and wars, whether or not they involve firing and killing, are acts of one side exerting power over another. These can be comprehensive or restrained, depending on the importance of the issue and the relative strength of the adversary. But once military war begins, all four dimensions will be weaponized to the maximum extent. As discussed in previous chapters, all the factors driving internal and external cycles tend to improve or worsen simultaneously. When things go wrong, there are more things to argue about, leading to a stronger tendency towards conflict. This is human nature, and it's why we have large cycles that oscillate between good and bad times. Total war usually occurs when there are existential problems (so vital to the survival of the state that people are willing to fight and sacrifice for them) that cannot be resolved peacefully. The ensuing war will clearly define which side will prevail and hold hegemony in subsequent affairs. This clarity about who sets the rules then becomes the basis of the new international order. The following chart shows the cycles of internal and external peace and conflict in Europe since 1500, reflected in the number of deaths they caused. As you can see, there are three major cycles of conflict, each averaging about 150 years. While large civil wars and foreign wars last only a short time, they are often the culmination of long-term conflicts that led to their occurrence. Although the First and Second World Wars were each driven by classic cycles, they were also interconnected. As you can see, each cycle consists of a relatively long period of peace and prosperity (e.g., the Renaissance, the Enlightenment, and the Industrial Revolution), which sowed the seeds for terrible and violent foreign wars (e.g., the Thirty Years' War, the Napoleonic Wars, and the two World Wars). Both periods of prosperity (peace and fortune) and periods of decline (depression and war) affect the entire world. Not all nations prosper when the dominant power is prosperous, because some nations achieve this at the expense of others. As you continue reading, remember these two things about war: 1) it will not go as planned, and 2) it will be far worse than imagined. It is for these reasons that many subsequent principles concern methods for avoiding hot war. Nevertheless, hot wars will occur for both good and bad reasons. To be clear, while I believe most wars are tragic and fought for absurd reasons, some wars are worth fighting because the consequences of not fighting (e.g., loss of freedom) are unacceptable. As I explained in Chapter Two, after self-interest and survival, the pursuit of wealth and power is the most motivating factor for individuals, families, companies, nations, and peoples. This is because wealth equates to power—the ability to build military force, control trade, and influence other countries—and domestic and military strength are interdependent. Purchasing guns (military power) requires money, as does purchasing butter (domestic social spending needs). When a nation fails to provide enough of either, it becomes vulnerable to opposition both domestically and internationally. Through studying dynasties and European empires, I have learned that financial strength—the ability to outspend rivals—is one of the most important strengths a nation can possess. This is how the United States defeated the Soviet Union in the Cold War. Spend enough money in the right way, and you don't have to go to war. Long-term success depends on maintaining "guns" and "butter" without engaging in excessive behavior that leads to your own decline. In other words, a nation must be financially strong enough to provide its people with a good standard of living and protection from external enemies. Truly successful nations can do this for 200 to 300 years. No nation can do it forever. Conflict arises when a dominant power begins to weaken, or a rising power begins to approach it in strength—or both. The greatest risk of military war lies in both sides possessing: 1) roughly equal military strength, and 2) irreconcilable and existential differences. The choice faced by opposing nations—to fight or to retreat—is extremely difficult. Both are costly—fighting costs lives and money, while retreating costs status because it demonstrates weakness and leads to a loss of support. When two competing entities each possess the ability to destroy the other, both must have an extremely high level of trust, certain that they will not suffer unacceptable harm or killing from the other. However, situations where the prisoner's dilemma is handled well are extremely rare. While there are no rules in international relations other than those imposed on themselves by the most powerful, certain approaches do indeed produce better results than others. Specifically, those approaches more likely to produce win-win outcomes are superior to those that lead to lose-lose outcomes. Therefore, the crucial principle is: to achieve more win-win results, one must consider the most important matters for both oneself and the other party in negotiations and know how to exchange them. Skillful cooperation that creates win-win relationships, increasing wealth and power while distributing them well, is far more rewarding and less painful than wars that result in one side conquering the other. The key to doing this is seeing things through the eyes of your opponent and clearly identifying and communicating your red lines (i.e., what cannot be compromised). Victory means gaining the most important things without losing the most important things; therefore, wars that cost far more in life and money than they gain are foolish. But "foolish" wars still occur, for reasons I will explain later. People are all too easily drawn into foolish wars for the following reasons: a) the prisoner's dilemma, b) the tit-for-tat escalation process, c) the weakening side believing that the cost of retreat is too high, and d) misunderstandings that arise when quick decisions are required. Rival powers often find themselves in a prisoner's dilemma; they need ways to assure the other that they will not attempt to kill them, lest the other strike first. A tit-for-tat escalation is dangerous because it requires each side to either escalate or lose what the opponent gained in the previous move; it's like a "game of chicken"—pushing too hard will result in a head-on collision. Untruthful and emotionally charged rhetoric increases the risk of foolish wars, so leaders are better off being truthful and thoughtful in explaining situations and how to respond (this is especially important in democracies, where public opinion is paramount). The worst-case scenario is when leaders are untruthful and emotional when dealing with their people, and even worse, when they take over the media. In general, the tendency to shift between win-win and lose-lose relationships occurs cyclically. People and empires are more likely to cooperate in good times and fight in bad times. When an existing great power is declining relative to a rising great power, it naturally tends to maintain the status quo or existing rules, while the rising great power seeks to change them to conform to the ever-changing realities on the ground. While I don't know the part about love in the saying "In love and in war, everything is fair," I do know the part about war is true. For example, in the American Revolutionary War, when the British lined up to fight and American revolutionaries fired at them from behind trees, the British thought it was unfair and complained. The revolutionaries won, they thought the British were foolish, and the cause of independence and freedom proved that changing the rules of war was justified. That's how it is. This leads to my final principle: Have power, respect power, and use power wisely. Having power is good because power always prevails over agreements, rules, and laws. When things come to a head, those who have the power to enforce their own interpretation of the rules and laws, or the power to overturn them, get what they want. Respect for power is important because fighting a war you can lose is unwise; it is preferable to negotiate the best possible solution (unless you want to be a martyr, which is usually for foolish self-interest rather than wise strategic reasons). Using power wisely is also important. Using power wisely doesn't necessarily mean forcing others to give you what you want—i.e., bullying them. It involves recognizing that generosity and trust are powerful factors in creating win-win relationships, which are far more beneficial than lose-lose ones. In other words, many times, using "hard power" is not the best approach, and using "soft power" is preferable. When considering how to use power wisely, deciding when to reach an agreement and when to go to war is also important. To do this, one party must envision how its power will change over time. It is preferable to use power to negotiate an agreement, implement an agreement, or wage war when one's power is at its peak. This means that if one's relative power is declining, it is advantageous to fight sooner; if one's power is rising, it is advantageous to fight later. If you're in a lose-lose situation, you must get rid of it somehow, ideally through separation, though war might also be an option. Handle your power wisely; generally, it's best not to flaunt it, as this can make others feel threatened and build their own threatening power, leading to escalation and endangering both sides. Power is usually best kept hidden, to be drawn when a fight breaks out. But sometimes, displaying power and threatening to use it is most effective in improving negotiating position and preventing conflict. Understanding what's most important and least important to the other side, especially what they're willing and unwilling to fight for, allows you to find a fair balance that both sides consider a solution to the dispute. While having power is generally desirable, not having power you don't need is also desirable. Maintaining power consumes resources, most importantly your time and money. Moreover, power comes with a heavy burden of responsibility. I am often surprised that those with less power can be far happier than those with more power. Now that we have covered the dynamics and principles driving cycles of external order and disorder (derived through the study of numerous cases), I would like to briefly review the case of World War II, as it provides a recent example of the typical dynamics of the transition from peace to war. While this is only one case, it clearly demonstrates how the convergence of three major cycles—the monetary and credit cycle, the internal order/disorder cycle, and the external order/disorder cycle—created the conditions for a catastrophic war and laid the foundation for a new world order. The stories of this period are fascinating in themselves, but they are particularly important because they offer lessons that help us think about the current situation and future possibilities. To help paint a picture of the 1930s, I will briefly review the key geopolitical events that led to the formal outbreak of war in Europe in 1939 and the attack on Pearl Harbor in 1941. Then, I will quickly glide over the war itself and the beginning of the new world order in 1945 when the United States was at the height of its power. The global depression following the Great Depression of 1929 led to massive internal conflicts over wealth in almost every country. This led them to more populist, authoritarian, nationalist, and militaristic leaders and policies. These shifts are either to the right or the left, varying in degree depending on national circumstances and the strength of their democratic or authoritarian traditions. In Germany, Japan, Italy, and Spain, extremely poor economic conditions and less-than-perfect democratic traditions led to extreme internal conflict, shifting towards right-wing populist/authoritarian leaders, much like the Soviet Union, which, similarly experiencing extreme conditions and lacking democratic experience, shifted towards left-wing populist/authoritarian leaders at different times. To understand what fascism is, we can examine three major choices a nation typically faces when selecting its mode of governance. In the United States, the debt problems following 1929 were devastating for American banks, restricting their lending worldwide and harming international borrowers. Deflationary depressions are debt crises triggered by debtors not having enough money to repay their debts. They inevitably lead to money printing, debt restructuring, and government spending programs that increase the supply of money and credit and devalue it. During periods of severe economic hardship and vast wealth inequality, revolutionary, large-scale redistribution of wealth often occurs. When carried out peacefully, this is achieved through substantial tax increases on the wealthy and increased money supply to devalue debtors' claims; when carried out violently, it is achieved through forced asset confiscation. In the United States and the United Kingdom, while a redistribution of wealth and political power occurred, capitalism and democracy were maintained. This was not the case in Germany, Japan, Italy, and Spain. Before a hot war begins, there is usually an economic war. In addition to internal economically motivated conflicts and the resulting political shifts, all these nations faced escalating external economic conflicts as they vied for a larger share of a shrinking economic pie. With power, rather than law, dominating international relations, Germany and Japan became more expansionist and began increasingly testing Britain, the United States, and France in their competition for resources and territorial influence. Before proceeding with the description of hot war, I want to elaborate on the common tactics used when economic capital instruments are weaponized. These tactics were common in the past and are still used today. These include asset freezes or confiscations—preventing an adversary from using or selling the foreign assets upon which they depend for survival. These measures are wide-ranging, ranging from asset freezes targeting specific groups within a country, such as the current US sanctions against the Iranian Revolutionary Guard or the US asset freeze on Japan in the early stages of World War II, to more severe measures, such as unilaterally refusing to repay debts or even directly confiscating a country's assets. Another strategy is blocking access to capital markets, preventing a country from entering its own or another country's capital markets. For example, in 1887, Germany hindered Russia's military buildup by prohibiting the purchase of Russian securities and debt. Furthermore, embargoes and blockades are also common tactics, aiming to weaken a target country or prevent it from obtaining essential resources by hindering trade in goods or services within its own borders, and in some cases, by blocking trade with neutral third parties. Typical examples include the US oil embargo against Japan during World War II and the blocking of Japanese ships through the Panama Canal. Similarly, preventing a target country's exports from flowing to other countries can cut off its revenue streams, as seen in the French blockade of Britain during the Napoleonic Wars. The Hot War Begins. In November 1937, Hitler secretly met with his senior officials and announced his plans for German expansion to acquire resources and unify the Aryan race. He then put these plans into action, first annexing Austria and then seizing oil-rich regions then belonging to Czechoslovakia. Europe and the United States watched warily, unwilling to be drawn into another war so quickly after the devastation of World War I. As with all wars, the unknown far outweighs the known because a) rival powers will only go to war when their strength is roughly equal (otherwise, it would be foolish suicide for the significantly weaker party), and b) there are too many possible actions and reactions to predict. The only thing known at the start of a hot war is that it may be extremely painful, even devastating. Therefore, wise leaders typically only go to war when the other side has forced them into a situation where they must either fight or be defeated by retreat. In March 1941, Congress passed the Lend-Lease Act, allowing the United States to lend or lease war materials to countries it deemed "critical to U.S. defense," including Great Britain and the Soviet Union. Helping allies was geopolitically and economically advantageous to the United States, as it profited greatly from selling weapons, food, and other goods to these quasi-allies struggling to maintain production during the war. However, the motivation was not purely mercenary. Britain's funds (i.e., gold) were depleted, so the United States allowed them to defer payments until after the war (and in some cases, waive payments altogether). While not an explicit declaration of war, the Lend-Lease Act effectively ended America's neutrality. When a nation is weak, its adversaries will exploit its weaknesses for their own gain. France, the Netherlands, and Great Britain all had colonies in Asia. Overstretched with its warfronts in Europe, the United States was unable to defend its colonies against Japan. Beginning in September 1940, Japan invaded several Southeast Asian colonies, starting with French Indochina, incorporating its so-called "Southern Resource Region" into its "Greater East Asia Co-Prosperity Sphere." In 1941, Japan seized the oil reserves of the Dutch East Indies. Japan's territorial expansion posed a threat to America's own Pacific ambitions. In July and August 1941, Roosevelt responded by freezing all Japanese assets in the United States, closing the Panama Canal to Japanese ships, and imposing an embargo on oil and natural gas. This cut off three-quarters of Japan's trade and 80% of its oil supply. Japan estimated its oil reserves would run out within two years. This left Japan with a choice: either back down or attack the United States. On December 7th and 8th, 1941, Japan launched a coordinated attack on American military forces at Pearl Harbor and in the Philippines. This marked the official outbreak of the Pacific War and drew the United States into the war in Europe. While Japan lacked a widely accepted plan to win the war, the most optimistic Japanese leaders believed that the United States would be defeated by fighting on two fronts and because its individualistic/capitalist political system was inferior to Japan's and Germany's authoritarian/fascist systems and their command military-industrial complexes. They also believed that their greater willingness to endure suffering and sacrifice for the nation was a crucial factor in determining which side would win. In war, the ability to endure suffering is even more important than the ability to inflict it. Just as noteworthy are typical international economic warfare strategies, so too are typical wartime economic policies within a nation. These include government control over virtually everything as the state shifts its resources from profit to war—for example, the government decides: a) which goods are permitted to be produced, b) which goods can be bought and sold in what quantities (rationing), c) which goods can be imported or exported, d) prices, wages, and profits, e) access to its own financial assets, and f) the ability to transfer personal funds abroad. Because war is costly, governments typically: g) issue large amounts of monetized debt; h) rely on non-credit currencies such as gold for international transactions because their credit is unacceptable; i) govern more authoritarianally; j) impose various types of economic sanctions on enemies, including cutting off their access to capital markets; and k) encounter enemies imposing these sanctions on them. When the United States joined the European and Pacific Wars after Pearl Harbor, most countries implemented typical wartime economic policies, with leaders adopting more authoritarian methods that enjoyed widespread public support. Market fluctuations during hot wars are heavily influenced by government control and the performance of various countries in the war, as the probability of victory or defeat changes. Many countries generally closed their stock markets, trapping stock investors and preventing them from accessing their capital. I should also point out that during wartime, currency and credit were generally not universally accepted among non-Allied nations because there were reasons to worry about whether the currency still had value. As mentioned earlier, gold—or in some cases silver or physical exchange—was the hard currency during wartime. In such times, prices and capital flows are often controlled, making it difficult to determine the true value of many things. Because losing a war typically results in the complete loss of wealth and power, the performance of stock markets that remained open during wartime largely depended on the performance of nations in key battles, as these outcomes altered the probabilities of victory or defeat for both sides. For example, the German stock market outperformed other countries in the early stages of World War II, when Germany had occupied territory and established military superiority, but lagged behind after the Allied powers, such as the United States and Great Britain, turned the tide of the war. After the Battle of Midway in 1942, Allied stock markets rose almost continuously until the end of the war, while Axis stock markets remained flat or declined. As shown in the chart, the German and Japanese stock markets were closed at the end of the war and reopened approximately five years later, almost worthless, while the US stock market was extremely strong. Protecting wealth during wartime is difficult because normal economic activity is restricted, traditionally safe investments are not secure, capital mobility is limited, and high taxes are levied when people and nations are fighting for survival. Protecting the wealth of existing holders is not a priority compared to the need to redistribute wealth to where it is most needed. As for investment, all debt should be sold and gold bought, because wars are financed through borrowing and printing money, which devalues ​​debt and currency, and people have reason to be unwilling to accept credit. Every world power has its glorious period, thanks to its unique circumstances and the nature of its character and culture (for example, its strong work ethic, intelligence, discipline, education, and other fundamental elements), but they all eventually decline. Some countries decline more smoothly and less traumatically than others, but they will still decline eventually. Traumatic declines can lead to the worst periods in history, when the great wars over wealth and power prove extremely costly in terms of both economy and human lives. Nevertheless, if a nation in its prosperous phase maintains productivity, income exceeding expenditure, ensures its institutions function well for the majority of its population, and manages to establish and sustain mutually beneficial relationships with its most important competitors, then this cycle need not unfold in this way. Many empires and dynasties lasted for centuries, and the United States, at 245 years old, has proven itself to be one of the longest-lived nations.</span></span></p></div></div>

data.title: 10 takeaways from Bitcoin Investor Week
data.description:
Author: Anthony Pompliano, Founder and CEO of Professional Capital Management; Translated by: Shaw Jinse Finance

Last week, I hosted Bitcoin Investor Week in New York City. Thousands of investors attended, listened to presentations from over 45 speakers, and made new friends through various side events and fun hours. Discussions covered a wide range of topics, from exploring the reasons for the recent decline in Bitcoin prices to assessing the possibility of deflation and interpreting the integration of artificial intelligence and Bitcoin.

Here are my main takeaways from this event: This isn't the first time Bitcoin investors have experienced this – many have held Bitcoin before and have weathered multiple drops of 50% or more, so the market didn't show significant panic throughout. Several media figures also pointed this out to me. This lack of panic makes me think that the current bear market is likely to be shallower and shorter than historical bear markets. Institutional investors have arrived – previous Bitcoin conferences have been filled with predictions of institutional investors joining the fray. This year's discussion focused on the progress made by institutional investors in the Bitcoin space, including the successful launch of Bitcoin exchange-traded funds (ETFs), the inclusion of Bitcoin on the balance sheets of numerous listed companies, the accelerated adoption of digital lending, the rapid growth of stablecoins, current tokenization initiatives, and the requirement for all traditional financial institutions to develop Bitcoin or cryptocurrency strategies. Bitcoin and Artificial Intelligence are Colliding—It's almost impossible to talk to a professional investor or entrepreneur without mentioning these two technologies. Clearly, Bitcoin and artificial intelligence represent the future of finance. There has been much speculation about how AI agents will conduct transactions or store value, and Bitcoin and stablecoins have naturally become popular answers. Deflation is a Major Risk—I personally believe that deflationary pressures from tariffs, deportations, artificial intelligence, and robotics are eroding the US economy. I asked many speakers and attendees if they agreed with my view, and the majority responded in the same way. Despite some lingering concerns about economic data and continued government money printing, there seems to be a general consensus that high inflation is not a problem in the short term. Financial advisors are holding or increasing their Bitcoin holdings—Bitwise CIO Matt Hougan explains that a recent survey showed 99% of financial advisors holding clients' Bitcoin assets are "holding" or "increasing" their Bitcoin holdings based on the recent price decline. This data indicates that the Registered Investment Advisor (RIA) channel is confident in Bitcoin's long-term return potential, thus strengthening client fund stickiness. Institutions holding Bitcoin ETFs haven't sold—BlackRock's Robert Mitchnick explains that most institutions holding Bitcoin ETFs have maintained their positions during the Bitcoin price decline. He believes BlackRock's client demand remains strong and that there is still significant room for growth in ETF allocations. Stablecoins Won't Disappear—Several speakers pointed out that the growth of stablecoins is remarkable, but more importantly, stablecoins are increasingly being integrated into the strategies of traditional institutions. Stablecoins appear to be the third crypto product, after Bitcoin and cryptocurrency exchanges, to truly find a product-market fit. No one dares to assert that Bitcoin has bottomed out—Although the price of Bitcoin may have fallen by 50%, few people agree when I ask them if they think the market has bottomed out. People remain cautious, seemingly because the painful lessons of past bear markets have forced them to prepare for even larger price drops. All eyes are on the Strategic Bitcoin Reserve (SBR)—The general consensus on the SBR is that while its establishment is welcome, the relatively small amount of Bitcoin purchased by governments is disappointing. If the market needs a new catalyst to drive an upward trend, then buying more Bitcoin for SBR might be a simple way to end the bear market. This momentum is unstoppable—not a single person I met at the conference believed the US government could balance its budget, stop printing money, or reduce the national debt. The prevailing view is that assets that benefit from currency devaluation (Bitcoin/gold/real estate, etc.) will perform exceptionally well over the next decade, especially given the current government's efforts to keep the economy hot and stimulate growth.
